slides.title: ACID: the promise behind reliable data
slides.body: When an app moves money, places an order, or saves a record, a database transaction needs a dependable outcome.

slides.title: ACID defines transaction guarantees
slides.body: ACID is a set of four properties that help databases handle changes safely, even with errors and concurrent users.

slides.title: The four properties, in plain language
slides.body: Atomicity makes a transaction all-or-nothing. Consistency preserves valid rules. Isolation separates concurrent work. Durability preserves commits.
items: C: valid state to valid state
I and D: safe concurrency and persistence

slides.title: Example: transferring $100
slides.body: A transfer debits one account and credits another. If the credit fails, atomicity rolls back the debit; a completed transfer remains after a crash.
items: Concurrent transfers do not corrupt balances

slides.title: ACID is about correctness, not speed
slides.body: Stronger guarantees can require locking, coordination, or logging. Systems sometimes trade some guarantees for availability or lower latency.
items: Choose based on the business risk

slides.title: Use transactions for related changes
slides.body: Wrap changes that must succeed together in one transaction: payments, inventory reservations, account updates, and order creation.

slides.title: ACID turns risky updates into dependable ones
slides.body: Atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability provide the foundation for data people can trust when failures and concurrency occur.
